title: Bosses warned of AI cyber onslaught
full_text:
The Australian Signals Directorate has joined with its Five Eyes partners to warn business leaders to urgently prepare their IT networks for an onslaught of cyber attacks powered by frontier AI models.
In an advisory issued with its counterparts in the US, UK, Canada and New Zealand, ASD’s Australian Cyber Security Centre said advanced AI technology was now a “core business risk”, lowering the barriers for hackers and increasing the complexity and speed of attacks.
The warning comes after the US government ordered one of the country’s leading AI companies, Anthropic, to block access to its newest Mythos AI model, so powerful it can detect and weaponise software vulnerabilities.
Industry experts believe Chinese AI companies are about 6-12 months behind those in the US in developing frontier models, but when cutting-edge versions are publicly released they will be available to users worldwide.
The Five Eyes advisory said the capabilities of such frontier AI models were likely to exceed industry expectations and fundamentally transform offensive and defensive cyber capabilities.
It said the threat timeline was “not years - it is months”. “Cyber risk can no longer be treated as a purely technical issue. This is a core business risk and leadership responsibility,” the advisory said. “Boards and executives should ensure cyber resilience is in place and works under pressure. It is not enough to have controls. Leaders must be confident those controls will perform during a real incident.
“This requires reassessing long-standing trade-offs and using AI deliberately to strengthen defence – not just improve efficiency.” The agencies said “new and previously unknown vulnerabilities will emerge, including zero-day vulnerabilities”, providing back-doors into systems previously thought to be secure.
They said IT systems needed to be “secure by design”, and companies needed to rely on multiple defences. Companies also needed to address their operational, financial and reputational exposure to successful system breaches, the agencies said.
Businesses are advised to limit unnecessary system access, prioritise security updates, get rid of unsupported legacy systems, and test their response plans.
The advisory also urges companies to match the rapid uptake of AI by hackers to ensure they are as prepared as possible for the new era of cyber threats.
“Organisations that integrate AI tools into their security operations can detect vulnerabilities earlier, improve software quality, monitor unusual behaviour, and respond faster to incidents – reducing both cost and impact of incidents. Success will not come from having the most tools. It will come from getting the basics right, acting quickly, and integrating cyber security into core business strategy.” The agencies said cybersecurity risk was being transformed so rapidly that businesses needed to be prepared to withstand evolving threats. “Cyber resilience is not an IT issue – it is central to operational continuity and market trust. Leaders who act now will reduce exposure, strengthen resilience, and build confidence with customers, partners, and investors. Those who delay will face growing and avoidable risk.”
